title: Western Military Doctrine Faces a Conceptual Crisis

source.original: General Valerii Zaluzhnyi on the Impossibility of Operational-Level Tasks Due to Technological Progress — Thread by Giorgi Revishvili

body_html:
NATO's military doctrine has been built, refined, and rehearsed around one central idea: that well-trained, technologically superior forces can achieve decisive results through combined-arms maneuver at operational depth. AirLand Battle, Full Spectrum Operations, Multi-Domain Operations — the names change, but the core assumption endures. Zaluzhnyi's battlefield-tested assessment strikes directly at that assumption.

If operational-level maneuver is no longer viable against a peer adversary equipped with modern ISR and precision fires, then the conceptual foundation of Western warfighting is in trouble. Force structures designed around armored brigade combat teams, deep-strike aviation, and rapid exploitation forces may be optimized for a form of warfare that the contemporary battlefield no longer supports.

This is not merely a theoretical problem. Procurement decisions worth hundreds of billions of dollars — next-generation tanks, infantry fighting vehicles, attack helicopters, deep-strike missiles — are predicated on the assumption that these systems will be employed in mobile, maneuver-centric operations. If those operations are impossible against a competent, technologically equipped adversary, the investment calculus changes dramatically.

Zaluzhnyi's credibility on this point is difficult to dismiss. He is not a commentator or an analyst — he is the commander who attempted to execute exactly these operations and concluded, from direct experience, that they cannot be done under current technological conditions. His authority derives from command responsibility in the largest European land war since 1945.

Western defense establishments must now grapple with an uncomfortable possibility: that their doctrinal frameworks require not incremental updating but fundamental reconception. The alternative — continuing to plan for a style of warfare that the battlefield has already rejected — carries risks that no alliance can afford.
